subject: Physics
options: Slope of the straight line get more steep
solution:
Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by,

eVs=hν-Wo, where Wo is the work function.

⇒Vs=hceλ-Woe, where c=νλ.

Intensity does not affect the frequency and work function of a material. Therefore, the graph does not change with increasing intensity.

subject: Physics
solution:
From photoelectric effect equation, we can write E=ϕ+Kmax

and work function can be written as ϕ=hcλ0.

Also, maximum kinetic energy Kmax=eV0

For first case:

8e=hcλ-hcλ0   ... (i)

For second case:

2e=hc3λ-hcλ0    ... (ii)

After using equation (ii) in (i), we get

4hc3λ-hcλ0=hcλ-hcλ0

⇒λ0=9λ

vpvα=4

subject: Physics
solution:
From the photoelectric equation,

eV0=hcλ−ϕ

Using this equation for the given two cases,

eV1=hc280 nm−ϕ

eV2=hc400 nm−ϕ

On subtracting,

eV1−V2=1240120280×400                   =1.3 eV

⇒V1−V2=1.3 V

solution: Comparing Einstein's equation Kmax=hv-hv0, with y=mx+c, we get slope, m=h, which is Planck’s constant.

subject: Physics
options: Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Both statement I and statement II are correct
solution:
Stopping potential depends on frequency of incident light and nature of the emitter material. It is independent of the power of the light source.

Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons is stated asKEmax=hν-ϕ=hcλ-ϕ, Where, h is the Planck’s constant, ν is the frequency, λ is wavelength of incident light and ϕ is the work function of the metal surface.

Thus, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron depends on the wavelength of the incident light.

So both statements I and II are correct

subject: Physics
solution:
Initial velocity of electron, 

v=v0i^+v0j^⇒u=v02+v02=v02

Initial de Broglie wavelength associated with electron, 

λ0=hp=hmv02

now,

v=u+at
Velocity as a function of time =v0i^+v0j^+eE0mtk^
so wavelength λ=hm2v02+e2E02m2t2
λ=λ01+e2E022m2v02t2

subject: Physics
solution:
The power of the incident light is P=25  W cm-2×25 cm2
=625 W
Energy incident per second, E=hcλ=625 J
Momentum incident per second, p=Ec=hλ=625c
∴ Total momentum transferred to the surface =625c×40×60
=5×10-3 N s

=6.6×10-34×6×10141.6×10-19-2

solution:
Intensity of light $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{nh} \nu}{\mathrm{A}}$ Here $\mathrm{n}$ is no. of photons per unit time. $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{IA}}{\mathrm{h} \nu}$ so on increasing frequency $v, \mathrm{n}$ decreases taking intensity constant. $\mathrm{k}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} v-\phi$
So on increasing $v$, kinetic energy increases.

subject: Physics

subject: Physics
solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength λ = h2m(K.E.)

where, h and K.E. are constant

λ∝1m

m= Mass of the particle

Thus, with increasing mass of particle de-Broglie wavelength decreases.

Order of mass me<mp<mn<mα

Order of de-Broglie wavelength is λa<λn<λp<λe

solution:

PowerP is given as, P=Et=nhcλt

From the above equation, the number of photons is n=Pλthc

=0.5×103×660×10-9×60×10-36.6×10-34×3×108

Hence, number of photons in the pulse are=100×1018=1020.

solution:
According to Bohr’s atomic model
mvr=nh2π
And the de-Broglie wavelength
λ=hmv
From the above two equations we can write, λ=2πrn
In the 2nd excited state
λ=2πr3
∴  λ=23π×4.65  Å=9.7 Å

subject: Physics
solution:
Given, $\lambda_1=4972 Å$
and $\lambda_2=6216 Å$
and $\mathrm{I}=3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$
Intensity associated with each wavelength
$$
=\frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=1.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}
$$
Work function $\phi=\mathrm{hv}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\frac{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right)\left(3 \times 10^8\right)}{\lambda} \\
&=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{\lambda} \mathrm{ev}
\end{aligned}
$$


for different wavelengths
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\phi_1=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{\lambda_1}=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{4972}=2.493 \mathrm{eV} \\
=3.984 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\
\phi_2=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{\lambda_2}=\frac{12.4 \times 10^3}{6216}=1.994 \mathrm{eV} \\
=3.184 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}
\end{array}
$$
Work function for metallic surface $\phi=2.3$ $\mathrm{eV}$ (given)
$\phi_2 < \phi$
Therefore, $\phi_2$ will not contribute in this process.
Now, no. of electrons per $\mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}=$ no. of photons per $\mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}$
no. of electrons per $\mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{3.984 \times 10^{-19}} \times 10^{-4} \\
&\left(\because 1 \mathrm{~cm}^2=10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2\right)=0.45 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}
$$
So, the number of photo electrons liberated
in 2 sec.
$=0.45 \times 10^{12} \times 2$
$=9 \times 10^{11}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Since the initial momentum of the system is zero. To keep the final momentum zero, the momentum of both parts should be equal and opposite in direction.

P→1=P→2

Here P→ is momentum

De-Broglie wavelength is given by λ=hP

Hence, both will have same de Broglie wavelength.

Therefore, λ1:λ2=1:1.

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Pressure }=\frac{I}{C}=\frac{F}{A} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{360}{10^{-4} \times 3 \times 10^8}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{A} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^2=0.02 \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Initially, energy of electron =+3eV

finally, in 2nd  excited state,

energy of electron =-(13.6eV)32

=-1.51eV

Loss in energy is emitted as photon,

So, photon energy hcλ=4.51eV

Now, photoelectric effect equation

KEmax=hcλ-ϕ=4.51-hcλth

=4.51eV-12400eVA4000A

=1.41eV

solution:

λ=h2mE

In the second case the kinetic energy is K=E4.

Let the de-Broglie wavelength be λ'.

The value of wavelength is

λ'=h2mE4=2h2mE=2λ

subject: Physics
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, hν=ϕ+eV0  .

⇒V0=hνe-ϕe  ......1

At ν=4×1014 Hz, V0=0.

Putting the value in equation 1, we get

 0=hve-ϕe

So, Work function (ϕ)=hνe  in eV
=6.63×10-34×4×10141.6×10-19
=1.66 eV
